@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 2, label : 'Customer ID' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 2, label : 'Customer ID' }] 
CustomerId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 3, label : 'Material' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 3, label : 'Material' }] 
Material, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 4, label : 'Price' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 4, label : 'Price' }] 
Price, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 5, label : 'Delivery Date' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 5, label : 'Delivery Date' }] 
DeliveryDate, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 6, label : 'Delivery Status' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 6, label : 'Delivery Status' }] 
DeliveryStatus 
}

Projection View for Billing Details

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Billing Details' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
define root view entity ZC_T_BILLING_S 
provider contract transactional_query as projection on ZI_T_BILLING_S 
{ 
@UI.facet: [{ id: 'Billingdetails',  
position: 1,  
label: 'Billing Details',  
type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE  
} ] 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 1, label : 'Billing Id' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 1, label : 'Billing Id' }] 
key BillingId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 2, label : 'Customer Id' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 2, label : 'Custoomer Id' }] 
CustomerId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 3, label : 'Delivery Id' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 3, label : 'Delivery Id' }] 
DeliveryId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 4, label : 'Material' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 4, label : 'Material' }] 
Material, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 5, label : 'Price' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 5, label : 'Price' }] 
Price 
}

STEP 4: Define Behavior Definitions
 Delivery BO behavior

  • Enable CRUD operation
  • Declare a non-factory action

This Action is responsible for creating Billing data and updating delivery status

managed implementation in class zbp_i_t_delivery_s unique; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZI_T_DELIVERY_S //alias <alias_name> 
persistent table zte_t_delivery_s 
lock master 
authorization master ( instance ) 
//etag master <field_name> 
{ 
create; 
update; 
delete; 
action create_delivery result [1] $self; 
mapping for zte_t_delivery_s 
{ 
DeliveryId = delivery_id; 
CustomerId = customer_id; 
Material = material; 
Price = price; 
DeliveryDate = delivery_date; 
DeliveryStatus = delivery_status; 
} 
}

Billing BO behavior

  • Enable CRUD operations
  • use Late Numbering for auto-generating Billing Id
managed implementation in class zbp_i_t_billing_s unique; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZI_T_BILLING_S //alias <alias_name> 
persistent table zte_t_billing_s 
lock master 
authorization master ( instance ) 
late numbering 
//etag master <field_name> 
{ 
create; 
update; 
delete; 

mapping for zte_t_billing_s 
{ 
BillingId = billing_id; 
DeliveryId = delivery_id; 
CustomerId = customer_id; 
Material = material; 
Price = price; 
} 
}

STEP 5: Define Projection behavior 

Delivery BO projection behavior

projection; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZC_T_DELIVERY_S //alias <alias_name> 
{ 
use create; 
use update; 
use delete; 
use action create_delivery; 
}

Billing BO projection behavior

projection; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZC_T_BILLING_S //alias <alias_name> 
{ 
use create; 
use update; 
use delete; 
}

STEP 6:  Implement Behavior Logic

Delivery Class

CLASS lhc_ZI_T_DELIVERY_S DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler. 

PRIVATE SECTION. 
METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION 
IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR zi_t_delivery_s RESULT result. 

METHODS create_delivery FOR MODIFY 
IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION zi_t_delivery_s~create_delivery RESULT result. 
ENDCLASS.
 
CLASS lhc_ZI_T_DELIVERY_S IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD get_instance_authorizations. 
ENDMETHOD. 

METHOD create_delivery. 
READ ENTITIES OF ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
IN LOCAL MODE 
ENTITY ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
FIELDS ( DeliveryId CustomerId Material Price ) 
WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys ) 
RESULT DATA(lt_delivery). 
LOOP AT lt_delivery INTO DATA(ls_delivery). 

"Create Billing Entry 
MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_T_BILLING_S 
ENTITY ZI_T_BILLING_S 
CREATE 
FIELDS ( DeliveryId CustomerId Material Price ) 
WITH VALUE #( 
( 
%cid = 'BILL1' 
DeliveryId = ls_delivery-DeliveryId 
CustomerId = ls_delivery-CustomerId 
Material = ls_delivery-Material 
Price = ls_delivery-Price 
) 
) 
FAILED DATA(ls_failed) 
REPORTED DATA(ls_reported). 

IF ls_failed IS NOT INITIAL. 
reported = CORRESPONDING #( ls_reported ). 
RETURN. 
ENDIF. 

"Update Delivery Status 
MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
IN LOCAL MODE 
ENTITY ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
UPDATE FIELDS ( DeliveryStatus ) 
WITH VALUE #( 
( 
DeliveryId = ls_delivery-DeliveryId 
DeliveryStatus = 'DELIVERED' 
) 
). 
ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

Billing Class

CLASS lhc_ZI_T_BILLING_S DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler. 
PRIVATE SECTION. 
METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION 
IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR zi_t_billing_s RESULT result. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS lhc_ZI_T_BILLING_S IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD get_instance_authorizations. 
ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS lsc_ZI_T_BILLING_S DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_saver. 
PROTECTED SECTION. 
METHODS adjust_numbers REDEFINITION. 
METHODS cleanup_finalize REDEFINITION. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS lsc_ZI_T_BILLING_S IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD adjust_numbers. 
SELECT FROM ZTE_T_Billing_S FIELDS MAX( billing_id ) INTO (ls_mat_num). 
DATA(lv_num) = ls_mat_num+3(4). 
LOOP AT mapped-zi_t_billing_s ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_material>). 
lv_num += 1. 
<fs_material>-BillingId = |BIL{ lv_num ALPHA = IN WIDTH = 4 }|. 
ENDLOOP. 
ENDMETHOD. 
METHOD cleanup_finalize. 
ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

STEP 7: Service Defination

Expose Both Behavior objects

@EndUserText.label: 'Delivery and billing details' 
define service ZSD_T_DETAILS { 
expose ZC_T_DELIVERY_S; 
expose ZC_T_BILLING_S; 
}

STEP 8 : Service Binding

  • create service binding
  • Activate and publish

STEP 9: RESULT

Create a record and click on the Action Button the delivery Status will be updated and Billing records will be created 

Billing details when Action is trigged and Delivery status updated to Delivery

Conclusion

Cross Business Object operations in RAP are essential for building real-world enterprise applications. While RAP abstracts much of the complexity, designing such interactions requires a clear understanding of:

  • Transactional behavior
  • BO independence vs coordination
  • Framework-driven data consistency By leveraging RAP Actions and EML, we can safely orchestrate multi-BO updates without compromising on clean architecture or data integrity.

This example demonstrates how a simple Delivery trigger can drive Billing creation, showcasing a scalable pattern that can be extended to more complex business processes.

entry.title: OData $batch Deleting Records Across Multiple Tables in Single Transaction
entry.content:
Introduction

 

Instead of sending each operation individually, OData batch processing enables you to transmit several operations (such as create, update, or delete) in a single request. This implies that you can use a single HTTP call to the $batch endpoint in SAP Gateway to execute actions on several tables at once.

For instance, you can make a single request to remove both a header record and the item records that are associated with it. When several records need to be managed as a single unit, this is helpful.

Batch processing enhances performance by minimizing network calls and ensures data consistency through change sets, which execute all operations as a single transactional unit. In case of any failure, the entire transaction is rolled back, thereby preventing partial data modifications.

Business Scenario

In the Manage Supplier Master application, a supplier’s complete data is not stored in a single table. Instead, it is distributed across multiple tables:

  • Basic Data (Supplier Name, Type)
  • Address Data
  • Identification Data (PAN, GST, etc.)
  • Purchasing Organization Data

Problem Statement

In the Manage Supplier Master application, when a user deletes a supplier, the system must remove all associated records from multiple backend tables, including basic details, address information, identification records, and purchasing organization data.

However, handling these deletions individually leads to several challenges:

  • Multiple HTTP calls are triggered for each table deletion
  • Increased network latency and poor performance
  • Risk of partial deletion (e.g., one table deleted, others not)
  • Data inconsistency across the system.

Prerequisites:

Before you implement batch delete in OData, make sure the following things are ready:

1. OData(V2) Service Setup: 

You should already have an SAP Gateway OData (v2) service created and registered.
All required tables (like ZTable1 , ZTable2) must be exposed as entity sets and should support DELETE operation.
Also, make sure runtime objects (Data Provider Class and Model) are generated using SEGW or CDS.

2. Entity Set Configuration:

Each table should be defined as an entity set with a key field.
In the service metadata (EDMX), batch processing ($batch) is available by default in OData v2.

3. Methods Implementation:

In the Data Provider Class:

  • Implement DELETE_ENTITY method for each entity (table).
  • Also implement:
    • CHANGESET_BEGIN
    • CHANGESET_PROCESS
    • CHANGESET_END

These help handle batch operations properly.

By default, only one delete is allowed per change set.
If you want multiple deletes in one go (same transaction), you need to enable deferred mode .

  METHOD /iwbep/if_mgw_appl_srv_runtime~changeset_begin.
    cv_defer_mode = abap_true.
  ENDMETHOD.

Why do we use deferred mode in OData batch processing?

Normal Behavior (Without Deferred Mode)

By default in SAP Gateway:

  • Each operation in a change set is handled individually
  • Only one DELETE/UPDATE/CREATE is allowed per change set
  • Each operation is processed immediately

So we cannot group multiple deletes in one transaction

With Deferred Mode

When you enable deferred mode:

  • All operations in the change set are collected first
  • Then they are processed together in CHANGESET_PROCESS
  • You can handle multiple deletes/updates in one go
  • Deferred mode ensures that all operations in a batch are executed sequentially in a controlled manner without interruption, as part of a single transaction.

This allows you to treat them as one logical unit (single LUW)

Implementation

Step 1:   Creating Database table

Zdt_mini_akbasic( basic information table for the supplier) 

Zdt_mini_ak_add( address table for the supplier) 

Zdt_mini_a_ident( Identity table for the Supplier )

Zdt_mini_a_porg( Purchase organization table )

Step 2:  Now need to create the OData project with all the tables/Structure

METHOD /iwbep/if_mgw_appl_srv_runtime~changeset_process.

  DATA: ls_changeset_request  TYPE /iwbep/if_mgw_appl_types=>ty_s_changeset_request,
        ls_changeset_response TYPE /iwbep/if_mgw_appl_types=>ty_s_changeset_response,
        lv_supplier_no        TYPE zde_mini_snumber,
        lv_request_line       TYPE string,
        ls_header             TYPE /iwbep/s_mgw_name_value_pair,
        lo_busi_exception     TYPE REF TO /iwbep/cx_mgw_busi_exception,
        lv_msg                TYPE string.

  LOOP AT it_changeset_request INTO ls_changeset_request.

    TRY.
        IF ls_changeset_request-operation_type = text-001.

          " Read each request header (name/value) and look for DELETE line
          LOOP AT ls_changeset_request-request_headers INTO ls_header.
             lv_request_line = |{ls_header-name} {ls_header-value}|.

            " Try to extract SupplierNumber from this line
            FIND PCRE 'SuppilerNumber=''([^'']+)''' " New syntax for Regular Expression
                 IN lv_request_line
                 SUBMATCHES lv_supplier_no.
            IF lv_supplier_no IS NOT INITIAL.
              EXIT. " Stop searching headers once we found the key
            ENDIF.
          ENDLOOP.

          IF lv_supplier_no IS INITIAL.
            MESSAGE e000(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
            lo_busi_exception = NEW /iwbep/cx_mgw_busi_exception( ).
            lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
              iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
              iv_msg_text =  CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
            ).
            RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
          ENDIF.

          " Delete from Basic Data
          IF sy-subrc = 0.
            DELETE FROM zdt_mini_akbasic
              WHERE suppiler_number = lv_supplier_no.
          ELSE.
            MESSAGE e002(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
            lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
            iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
            iv_msg_text = CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
            ).
            RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
          ENDIF.

          " Delete from Address
          IF sy-subrc = 0.
            DELETE FROM zdt_mini_ak_add
              WHERE suppiler_number = lv_supplier_no.
          ELSE.
            MESSAGE e003(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
            lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
            iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
            iv_msg_text = CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
            ).
            RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
          ENDIF.

          " Delete from Identification
          IF sy-subrc = 0.
            DELETE FROM zdt_mini_a_ident
              WHERE suppiler_number = lv_supplier_no.
          ELSE.
            MESSAGE e004(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
            lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
            iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
            iv_msg_text = CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
            ).
            RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
          ENDIF.

          " Delete from Purchasing Org
          IF sy-subrc = 0.
            DELETE FROM zdt_mini_a_porg
              WHERE suppiler_number = lv_supplier_no.
          ELSE.
            MESSAGE e005(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
            lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
            iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
            iv_msg_text = CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
            ).
            RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
          ENDIF.

        ENDIF.

        " Maintain operation number in the response
        ls_changeset_response-operation_no = ls_changeset_request-operation_no.
        APPEND ls_changeset_response TO ct_changeset_response.
      CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_root).
        MESSAGE e000(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
        lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
        iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
        iv_msg_text = CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
        ).
        RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
    ENDTRY.

    CLEAR: ls_changeset_request, ls_changeset_response, lv_supplier_no, lv_request_line.

  ENDLOOP.

ENDMETHOD.
  method /IWBEP/IF_MGW_APPL_SRV_RUNTIME~CHANGESET_END.
    COMMIT WORK.
  endmethod.

What COMMIT WORK Actually Does

  • Saves all successful operations to database
  • Ends the LUW (Logical Unit of Work)
  • Makes changes visible to other users.

Output:

--batch
Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary=changeset


--changeset
Content-Type: application/http
Content-Transfer-Encoding: binary


DELETE zakBasicSet(SupplierNumber='S0038') HTTP/1.1


--changeset--
--batch--

Conclusion:

  1. Once the $batch request is received, SAP Gateway identifies the changeset and starts processing it as a single transaction (LUW).
  2. The framework then reads each operation inside the changeset and calls the respective method.
  3. All operations inside the changeset are executed sequentially (one by one) but within the same transaction context.
  4. The backend logic performs all related table deletions, but no database commit happens immediately during execution.
  5. After all operations are successfully completed, COMMIT WORK is triggered automatically; if any operation fails, the entire changeset is rolled back, ensuring no partial data is saved.

entry.title: RAP Unmanaged Implementation: Draft Enablement, Late Numbering, and Associations Explained.
entry.content:
Introduction.

SAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a powerful framework for building modern, scalable applications. While managed scenarios offer built-in handling of persistence and standard operations, unmanaged scenarios give developers greater flexibility to implement custom business logic and control the complete data flow manually.

In this blog, we will explore how to build an unmanaged RAP Business Object, focusing on key concepts such as Late Numbering, Draft Handling, and Header-Item associations.

LATE NUMBERING:

           In Late Numbering the primary key (usually ID) of a business object is not generated at the time of creation, but instead assigned later during the save (persist) phase, this is typically handled inside the SAVE method of the behavior implementation class.
So initially:

  • Temporary keys are used during transaction processing.
  • Final keys are generated only when data is committed to the database.

Late numbering is useful in scenarios where:

  • Number depends on final validation or logic
    Example: Generate ID only after all validations pass
  • Avoid number gaps
    If numbering happens early and transaction fails → gaps occur. Late numbering ensures numbers are assigned only for successful transactions

How It Works (Conceptually)

  • User creates record → temporary key assigned
  • Data stays in transactional buffer

During SAVE:

  • Generate final number
  • Replace temporary key with actual key
  • Save to database

DRAFT:

Draft handling allows users to save incomplete data without activating it in the database.
It enables:

  • “Save as Draft”
  • “Edit Later”
  • “Activate when ready”

This is widely used in Fiori applications.

Why Do We Use Draft?

  • User convenience
       Users can partially fill data and come back later.
  • Avoid data loss
       Work is not lost if session ends.

How Draft Works (Flow):

  • User creates or edits data → stored in Draft table.
  • Draft data is isolated from active data.
  • User can:
    1. Edit draft
    2. Discard draft
    3. Activate draft

On Activate:

  • Draft data moves to active table.
  • Business logic (validations, determinations) is triggered.

Number Assignment in Draft with Late Numbering:
What happens to the ID in Draft?

When we use Late Numbering + Draft, the actual (final) key is NOT assigned in the draft table.

Instead:

  • A temporary key (UUID or %CID) is used internally.
  • The real number is assigned only during Activate (SAVE).

There will be two phase:
      Draft Phase and Activation Phase.
During Draft (Create / Edit).

  • No final number is generated
    RAP uses:
    1. Temporary key (%CID) in backend processing
    2. Draft UUID (technical key) in draft tables
    3. The business key field is usually:
          Empty OR Filled with a placeholder (optional, but not final)

So draft data is stored without the actual business number

During Activation (Save)

  • SAVE method is triggered
  • You generate the final number
    System:
    1. Replaces temporary key with actual key
    2. Moves data from draft → active table

SCENARIO:

In this application, we have a Product (Header) table (ZGN_T_PRODUCTS) and an Order (Item) table (ZGN_T_ORDERS) linked through productid. Users can create and save Product records independently, while Orders can be created later based on the product. The requirement is to generate both Product ID and Order ID only during final save (activation) using late numbering. Users should also be able to save incomplete data as draft and complete it later. To handle custom logic, and manual persistence, an unmanaged RAP approach is used

1.Database tables:

@EndUserText.label : 'Product table - Header'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_t_products {

  key client    : abap.clnt not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  productname   : abap.char(15);
  quantity      : abap.char(3);
  price         : abap.char(7);
  totalprice    : abap.char(10);
  lastchange    : timestampl;

}

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Order table - Item'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_t_orders {

  key client    : abap.clnt not null;
  key orderid   : abap.char(3) not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  orderstatus   : abap.char(10);
  location      : abap.char(20);

}

2. Created draft tables for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) entities to support draft functionality. These tables store intermediate data, allowing users to save incomplete records and activate them later.

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZGN_I_PRODUCTS'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_dft_prdts {

  key mandt     : mandt not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  key draftuuid : sdraft_uuid not null;
  productname   : abap.char(15);
  quantity      : abap.char(3);
  price         : abap.char(7);
  totalprice    : abap.char(10);
  lastchange    : timestampl;
  "%admin"      : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;

}
@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZGN_I_ORDERS'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_dft_order {

  key mandt       : mandt not null;
  key orderid     : abap.char(3) not null;
  key productid   : abap.char(3) not null;
  key draftuuid   : sdraft_uuid not null;
  parentdraftuuid : sdraft_uuid;
  orderstatus     : abap.char(10);
  location        : abap.char(20);
  "%admin"        : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;

}

3. Created an Interface view on top of these two database table. Defined a proper association between Product (Header) and Order (Item) using productid to establish the relationship.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Product interface view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZGN_I_PRODUCTS as select from ZGN_T_PRODUCTS
composition[1..*] of ZGN_I_ORDERS as _order
{
    key productid as Productid,
    productname as Productname,
    quantity as Quantity,
    price as Price,
    totalprice as Totalprice,
    lastchange as Lastchange,
    _order
}
@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Order interface view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZGN_I_ORDERS as select from zgn_t_orders
association to parent ZGN_I_PRODUCTS as _products
on $projection.Productid = _products.Productid
{
    key orderid as Orderid,
    key productid as Productid,
    orderstatus as Orderstatus,
    location as Location,
    _products
}

4.  Created a consumption CDS view on top of the interface views to expose the data for UI usage. Instead of using metadata extensions, all required UI annotations were directly maintained within the consumption view for simplicity.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Product consumption view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZGN_C_PRODUCTS
  provider contract transactional_query
   as projection on ZGN_I_PRODUCTS
{
       .facet: [{ position: 1,
    type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Product details' },
                  
                  {
                  position: 2,
                  type: #LINEITEM_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Order details',
                  targetElement: '_order'
                  } ]
                  
    
    .lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Product id' }]
    key Productid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 20, label : 'Product Name' }]
    Productname,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 30, label : 'Quantity' }]
    Quantity,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 40, label : 'Price' }]
    Price,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 50 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 50, label : 'Total price' }]
    Totalprice,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 60 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Last change' }]
    Lastchange,
    _order : redirected to composition child ZGN_C_ORDERS

}
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Order projection view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZGN_C_ORDERS as projection on ZGN_I_ORDERS
{
     .facet: [{ position: 1,
    type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Order details' }]
                  
    .lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Order id' }]
    key Orderid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 20, label : 'Product id' }]
    key Productid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 30, label : 'Order status' }]
    Orderstatus,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 40, label : 'Location' }]
    Location,
    /* Associations */
    _products : redirected to parent zgn_c_products
}

5. Created behavior definitions on top of the interface views for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) entities. Defined the required operations and enabled features like draft handling, associations, and late numbering as per our requirements.

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_gn_i_products unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;

define behavior for ZGN_I_PRODUCTS //alias <alias_name>
draft table zgn_dft_prdts
late numbering
lock master
total etag Lastchange
authorization master ( instance )

{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) Productid, Totalprice;
  association _order { create; with draft; }
  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft action Discard;
  draft determine action Prepare;

  determination total on modify { field Price; field Quantity; }
  side effects
  { field Price affects field Totalprice;
    field Quantity affects field Totalprice; }

  mapping for zgn_t_products
    {
      Productid   = productid;
      Productname = productname;
      Quantity    = quantity;
      Price       = price;
      Totalprice  = totalprice;
      Lastchange  = lastchange;
    }

}

define behavior for ZGN_I_ORDERS //alias <alias_name>
draft table zgn_dft_order
late numbering
lock dependent by _products
authorization dependent by _products
{
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly : update ) Orderid, Productid;
  association _products { with draft; }

  mapping for zgn_t_orders
    {
      Orderid     = orderid;
      Productid   = productid;
      Orderstatus = orderstatus;
      Location    = location;
    }

}

 6. Implemented the required business logic in the behavior pool class using a helper class. This includes handling late numbering, calculations, and manual data persistence for both Product and Order entities.

    In the helper class, created a dedicated method to handle late numbering logic. This method generates the Product ID and Order ID and is called from the behavior pool adjust_numbers method.

 METHODS late_num CHANGING mapped   TYPE tt_late
                              reported TYPE tt_report_late  .

 METHOD late_num.
    IF gt_tab IS NOT INITIAL.
      SELECT FROM zgn_t_products
      FIELDS
      MAX( productid )
      INTO (lv_max).
      LOOP AT gt_tab ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_max>).
        lv_max += 1.
        <fs_max>-productid = lv_max.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.

    IF gt_tab_item1 IS NOT INITIAL.
      SELECT FROM zgn_t_orders
     FIELDS
     MAX( orderid )
     INTO (lv_max_i).
      LOOP AT gt_tab_item1 ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_max_i>).
        lv_max_i += 1.
        <fs_max_i>-orderid = lv_max_i.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

     Called this method in the behavior pool class

  METHOD adjust_numbers.
    zcl_gn_helper_unmln=>get_inst(  )->late_num(
      CHANGING
        mapped   = mapped
        reported = reported
    ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD save.
    zcl_gn_helper_unmln=>get_inst(  )->save_data(
     CHANGING
       reported = reported
   ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Implemented Persistance Logic for Header and Item.

Implemented the create methods for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) in the behavior implementation.

  • In the header create method (create_data), the incoming entity data is mapped to an internal table using CORRESPONDING, which is later used for persistence.
  • In the item create method (create_item):
    1. The Product ID from the header is captured.
    2. Item data is extracted using %target.
    3. A loop is used to assign the Product ID to each item, maintaining the header- item association.
    4. Finally, the data is prepared for database insertion and mapped back to the response structure.

    This ensures that all order items are correctly linked to their respective product during creation.

%target is used in composition (Header → Item) scenarios to access the child entity data (Item records) that come along with the parent during a deep create.

 METHOD create_data.
    gt_tab = CORRESPONDING #( entities MAPPING FROM ENTITY ).
  ENDMETHOD.

METHOD create_item.
    DATA(lv_pid) = entities[ 1 ]-Productid.
    gt_tab_item = CORRESPONDING #( entities[ 1 ]-%target ).

    LOOP AT gt_tab_item ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_item>).
      <fs_item>-productid = lv_pid.
    ENDLOOP.

    APPEND VALUE zgn_t_orders( productid = <fs_item>-productid
                               orderid = <fs_item>-orderid
                               orderstatus = <fs_item>-orderstatus
                               location = <fs_item>-location )
                        TO gt_tab_item1.
    mapped-zgn_i_orders = CORRESPONDING #( entities ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Output:

During product creation, the Product ID is not assigned initially and remains empty in draft mode; it is generated during the save (activation) phase through late numbering.

After clicking on create:

Same applies for Order ID, which remains blank during creation and gets generated upon save

The below image shows the draft table entries for Orders, where the data is temporarily stored during the draft phase. At this stage, the Order ID is not yet finalized, and the system maintains a draft UUID to uniquely identify the record.

After clicking on save the order id gets generated.

The below screen shows the active data after clicking Save (activation). Both Product ID (Header) and Order ID (Item) are now successfully generated using late numbering. The Product and its associated Orders are persisted in the database with proper header-item linkage via productid.

Conclusion:

In this blog, we explored how to implement an unmanaged RAP Business Object with key features like Late Numbering, Draft Handling, and Header-Item association. We saw how both Product (Header) and Order (Item) records can be created in draft mode and how their IDs are generated only during the save (activation) phase using late numbering. By handling the logic in the behavior pool and helper class, we achieved full control over data persistence, and associations.

entry.title: Sales Configuration display by CDS with table function/HANA SQL for Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP
entry.content:
In that blog I want to share approach to display Sales Item Variant Configuration in CDS-way. The approach could be used both in new reporting technology (like Fiori RAP-reporting (managed and unmanaged), Embedded Analytics) and in classical (ALV classic Dynpro, SE16N, SE16H etc.)

Technically, the approach is using the following:

  • Table Function with HANA SQL with scalar and table variable declaration
  • Functions STRING_AGG for row concatenation, CAST for casting to target type, CASE for conditional logic in HANA SQL
  • Window function for grouping and splitting the logic inside SQL-procedure
  • Sorting inside database procedure

So, it can be used both for Sales Item Variant Configuration and as a cheat-sheet for HANA SQL in AMDP-classes.

The blog is dedicated to Natalya Yakimenko; really encouraging and supportive person especially for Analytical Tools in Big Data / HANA.

What is Sales Item Variant Configuration?

In sales order we can parametrize item (configure) with characteristics according to customer needs: width, length, color, chemicals, mechanicals and so on. The configuration – is what the customer wants; so, it could be not only exact values, but interval values also; for example, Carbon (C) between 1 and 2 %. Also, it could be several values: for example, RED, YELLOW color.

On the Figure 1 it is shown sample of exact and multiple values. LENGTH has exact value and Colour has multiple values.

Figure 1 Exact value with UOM for LENGTH and multiple values for Colour

 

On the Figure 2 it is shown interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration. Carbon (C) has interval from 1 to 2 percents and Hydrogen (H) should be strictly more than 5% (also pay attention to different decimals part on the screenshot).

Figure 2 Sample of Interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration

if you want more to know about variant configuration - you can check the links:

1) Introducing Variant Configuration ( https://learning.sap.com/courses/variant-configuration-in-sap-s-4hana-enterprise-management/introducing-variant-configuration ) (thanks to @Jelena_Perfiljeva  )

2) Variant Configuration (LO-VC) in SAP S/4HANA ( https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-members/variant-configuration-lo-vc-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/14234012 )

3) Help-documentation on LO-VC component (https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/61155ee7ed304ae8b66b7526555a6a07/1a40b953495bb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US&version=6.18.latest )

How to read the variant configuration programmatically for reporting purposes?

Classical approach with function

In table VBAP (sales items) there is a special field (VBAP-CUOBJ, or CDS I_SalesDocumentItem-ProductConfiguration ) that links Sales Item Data Model to Variant Configuration Data Model.

For classical reporting very often the function VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE was used. It is still good function, and can be used, however

  • it does not allow us to select by characteristics values
  • it hardly can be used inside new approaches like Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP-reporting
    DATA lt_CONFIGURATION     TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF conf_out.
    DATA lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF  api_value .

    CALL FUNCTION 'VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE'
      EXPORTING
        instance            = iv_cuobj                 
      TABLES
        configuration       = lt_CONFIGURATION                  
        et_conf_with_author = lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR               
      EXCEPTIONS
        instance_not_found  = 1
        OTHERS              = 2.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE ID sy-msgid TYPE sy-msgty NUMBER sy-msgno
        WITH sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4 INTO sy-msgli.
    ENDIF.

 

For Embedded Analytics and RAP-applications I decided to create two group of CDS (view entities):

  • Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with specifying master info about characteristics itself: data type, single/multiple etc.
  • CDS with table function which present any char in the one line.

 

CDS with values in each Row

The CDS (view entity) for Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with master data is the following. We could see that core tables are: IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The base part: 

define view entity ZI_CUOBJ_CLS300
  as select from ibin       as ibh
    inner join   ibinvalues as ibv    on ibh.in_recno = ibv.in_recno
    inner join   ibsymbol   as ibsymb on ibsymb.symbol_id = ibv.symbol_id
    inner join   cabn       as CABN   on  cabn.atinn = ibsymb.atinn
                                      and cabn.adzhl = '0000'


  association [1] to I_ClfnCharcDesc         as _CharDescr  on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID   = _CharDescr.CharcInternalID
                                                            and _CharDescr.Language           = 'E'
                                                            and _CharDescr.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'

  association [1] to ZI_CHARVALUE_MD as _CharValueH on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID =  _CharValueH.CHARCINTERNALID
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      =  _CharValueH.CHARCVALUE
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      <> ''

{
  key ibh.instance                      as cuobj,
  key ibh.in_recno                      as IN_RECNO,
  key ibv.in_segmcnt                    as IN_SEGMCNT,

      ibsymb.atinn                      as CHARCINTERNALID,
      cabn.atnam                        as CharHure,
      _CharDescr.CharcDescription       as CharcDescription,
      cabn.atfor                        as dt_type,
      cabn.anzst                        as NumOfChars,
      cabn.anzdz                        as NumOfDecimals,
      cabn.msehi                        as char_uom,
      cabn.atein                        as Single_Mark,
      ibsymb.atcod                      as IntervalType,

      ibsymb.atwrt                      as CharcValuestr,
      ibv.dec_value_from                as CHARCFROMDECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.dec_value_to                  as CHARCUPTODECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.date_from                     as CharcFromDate,
      ibv.date_to                       as CharcUptoDate,
      ibv.time_from                     as CharcFromTime,
      ibv.time_to                       as CharcUptoTime,
      _CharValueH.CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,


      /* associations */
      _CharValueH

}

 

Part: ZI_CHARVALUE_MD

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'MasterData for Char Value'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI CHARVALUE_MD
  as select from I_ClfnCharcValue as ValID
  association [0..1] to ZI_CharcValueDesc as _ValDesc     on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID    = _ValDesc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber = _ValDesc.CharcValuePositionNumber
                                                                  and _ValDesc.Language              = 'E'
                                                                  and _ValDesc.TimeIntervalNumber    = '0000'

  association [1..1] to I_ClfnCharcBasic          as _ClasCharBsc on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID     = _ClasCharBsc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and _ClasCharBsc.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'
{
  key ValID.CharcInternalID          as CHARCINTERNALID,
  key ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber as CharValuePos,
      _ClasCharBsc.Characteristic    as CharNamHuRe, --Human Readable
      ValID.CharcValue               as CHARCVALUE,
      _ValDesc.CharcValueDescription as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,
      
      ValID.CharcFromDecimalValue    as CHARC_DECVAL,
      
      _ValDesc,
      _ClasCharBsc
}

 

part: ZI_CharcValueDesc

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Characteristcis Value Desc'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI_CharcValueDesc 
    as select from cawnt as CharacteristicValueDesc
  
      association [0..1] to I_Language as _Language 
        on $projection.Language = 'E'
             
{
  key   cast ( CharacteristicValueDesc.atinn as atinn_no_conv preserving type ) as CharcInternalID,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.atzhl as CharcValuePositionNumber,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Language'
        @Semantics.language: true
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.spras as Language,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.adzhl as TimeIntervalNumber,
        @Semantics.text: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.atwtb as CharcValueDescription,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.aennr as ChangeNumber,
        @Semantics.businessDate.from: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datuv as ValidityStartDate,
        @Semantics.businessDate.to: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datub as ValidityEndDate,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.lkenz as IsDeleted,
        
        _Language
}

 

The result we could see like that (from SE16H).

We could get values, uom (unit of measure), and data type description for each characteristic from sales item variant configuration. We could use it for searching and data validations (during migration for example) also.

 

Figure 3 Sales Item Variant Configuration Sample

It is useful and informative, but it could lead to changes of cardinality in CDS-joins because we could have multiple values for some characteristics, also we need to do cast in each report/application and in case data type of characteristics is changed we need to change application. So, it would be better, for some cases, to get all values from one field. And it is possible with table function, as STRING_AGG is not working in CDS, but either in HANA SQL or ABAP.

 

Intervals could be like that (shown on figure)

 

Figure 4 Different types of intervals in Sales Item Variant Configuration

 

 

CDS with table function / amdp-procedure on HANA SQL

In table function we need do the following:

  • Format values (especially numeric ones) to their format from master data
  • Concatenate values into one field
  • For intervals it is needed to put special symbols: prefix, delimeter and postfix.

 

As in rows-oriented CDS it is using the following tables: VBAP, IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The idea is simple:

  • Target output type is CHAR with length 120 (of course can be longer, but for my purposes it was more than enough). (pay attention to the field ATWRT_STR in the table function below)
  • All values are selecting and casting to the target type depending on decimals in the characteristic’s master data.
  • If there are multiple values – it is concatenated in rows by function STRING_AGG
  • And for output purposes it is concatenated not only with values itself but with special sign (less (<), less or equal (<=), more or equal (=>), more) (pay attention to table declaration _t_interval_extstr and filling) (see figure 2 with Hydrogen value)
  • The procedure returns sorted data – so it can be used in applications for binary search purposes without sorting on the application’s side

 

Code sample for table function for sales variant configuration’s output

@EndUserText.label: 'SOI - Conf Cls300 - AGG mode'
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_INDEPENDENT

define table function ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
returns {
  slsh : vbeln_va;
  slsi : posnr_va;
  MATERIAL: matnr;
  Plant: werks_d;
  ItemDescr: arktx;
  PRODUCTCONFIGURATION: cuobj_va;
  
  ORDERQUANTITY: kwmeng;
  OrderQuantityUnit: vrkme;
  
  ATINN : atinn;
  atnam : atnam;
  ATWRT_STR: abap.char(120);
  }
implemented by method zcl_tf_soicls300_agg=>read_cls300_one_line;

 

Code sample for HANA SQL AMPD-procedure

CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    CLASS-METHODS read_cls300_one_line
        FOR TABLE FUNCTION ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
      .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD read_cls300_one_line BY DATABASE FUNCTION
        FOR HDB
        LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
        OPTIONS READ-ONLY
        USING vbap
              ibin
              ibinvalues
              ibsymbol
              cabn
              .


    -- https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-sqlscript-reference/
    -- ... table-variable-type-definition?locale=en-us

        declare _t_interval_extstr table(
            atcod "$ABAP.type( ibvalcod )",
            pre_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )",
            delim "$ABAP.type( char5 )",
            post_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )"
        );

        declare _mandt varchar(3) := session_context('CLIENT');

* see data element  domanin: IBVALCOD
*0   Empty Quantity (No Value Allowed)
*1   = Lower limit
*2   [ Lower limit, upper limit )
*3   [ Lower limit, upper limit ]
*4   ( Lower limit, upper limit )
*5   ( Lower limit, upper limit ]
*6   ( - Infinite, upper limit )
*7   ( - Infinite , upper limit ]
*8   ( Lower limit, infinite )
*9   [ Lower limit, infinite )


        _t_interval_extstr =
            select '1' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, '' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '2' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '3' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '4' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '5' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '6' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '7' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '8' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '9' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            ;


          _single_n_intervals =
        with _low_fromto_agg as (

          select
           ibval.mandt,
           ibval.in_recno,
           ibsym.atinn,
           cabn.atnam,

           cast( cabn.atfor as char(70) ) as atwrt,

           case cabn.atfor
              when 'CHAR' then
                 cast(
                    string_agg( ibsym.atwrt, '; ' )
                    as char (120) )

              WHEN 'NUM' then


*    begin of num - case (it consists of two parts: with intervals and strict equal)

               case -- ibsym.atcod
*    no intervals
                when ibsym.atcod = '1' or ibsym.atcod = '8' or ibsym.atcod = '9' then

                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                    when '0' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '1' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '2' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '5' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '10' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )

                  END

              else
*    with intervals
                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                   when '0' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,0)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '1' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,1)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '2' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,2)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then
                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,3)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then
                            cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,4)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )

                     WHEN '5' then

                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,5)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     WHEN '10' then
                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (21,10)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )
                     END

                 END


                END as atwrt_str


              from ibinvalues as ibval
                join ibsymbol as ibsym
             ON ibsym.symbol_id = ibval.symbol_id
             and ibsym.mandt = ibval.mandt

             join cabn as cabn
              on cabn.atinn = ibsym.atinn
              and cabn.adzhl = '0000'
              and cabn.mandt = ibsym.mandt


            join :_t_interval_extstr as interv
                on interv.atcod = ibsym.atcod


           where ibval.mandt = :_mandt

          group by ibval.mandt, ibval.in_recno, ibsym.atinn, ibsym.atcod, cabn.atnam, cabn.atfor, cabn.anzst, cabn.anzdz

        )

        select
          vbap.vbeln as slsh,
          vbap.posnr as slsi,
          vbap.matnr as material,
          vbap.werks as plant,
          vbap.arktx as itemdescr,
         vbap.cuobj as productconfiguration,

         vbap.kwmeng as orderquantity,
         vbap.vrkme as orderquantityunit,

         fromto_agg.atinn,
         fromto_agg.atnam,
         fromto_agg.atwrt_str

            from vbap as vbap

            inner join ibin       as ibh on vbap.cuobj = ibh.instance

            inner join _low_fromto_agg as fromto_agg on fromto_agg.in_recno = ibh.in_recno

            ;

        RETURN
            select * from :_single_n_intervals
            ORDER BY slsh, slsi, atinn
            ;

  eNDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

As a result we could see different types of values in one cell but with different formats (with taken into account decimals, multiple values, and interval type)

Figure 5 Result Output of HANA SQL procedure

 

Conclusion

In that blog I have used HANA SQL for data selecting and formatting for Sales Variant Configuration data model. Comments / additions / recommendations are welcomed both as about sales variant configuration and HANA SQL itself. Thank you.

For contacting with me: my linked.

HANA SQL help for aggregate functions could be found here.

entry.title: Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions – Monthly Series (Session 3)
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community and #ABAPlovers

My name is Sandra Solís, an ABAP Developer with a focus on ABAP, ABAP Cloud, and modern SAP development practices. I’m passionate about building clean, efficient solutions and continuously learning new technologies within the SAP ecosystem.

From my experience in SAP projects, learning sessions, and community events, the most valuable moments are always the same: Asking questions — any kind of question — and getting honest, practical answers from people who have been there.

For this reason, I’m launching the session 3 of AMA (Ask Me Anything) series.

This space is dedicated to all things ABAP:
classic ABAP, ABAP Cloud, RAP, performance tuning, clean code, debugging, real project challenges, and even career growth in SAP development.

You are invited to ask any questions you have, openly or anonymously. No question is too simple or too complex. Let’s make this a space for sharing real experiences, solving doubts, and learning together as a community.

You can drop your question in the comments below.

or you can ask anonymously here:  Ask Me Anything Champion - Session 3 ABAP

Submit your questions during this month, and selected ones will be answered in a follow-up  video recap. Looking forward to your questions 🚀

entry.title: Partner learning accelerator: SAP Certified - Backend Developer - ABAP Cloud
entry.content:
Getting ready for your next ABAP certification can feel like a lot.  Tight timelines, evolving concepts, and real project work happening at the same time.

That’s exactly why we designed the Partner Learning Accelerator for SAP Certified – Backend Developer – ABAP Cloud.

In this 6-hour live session, you’ll focus on what matters most.  Core ABAP concepts, the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model, and Clean Core principles in SAP S/4HANA.  Plus, practical guidance you can connect directly to your day-to-day work.

Think of it as a structured checkpoint.  You validate what you know, close the gaps, and move into the exam with more clarity.

If you’ve worked with ABAP before and want to take that next step, this session gives you a clear path forward.

Register now and move closer to certification with confidence.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jul/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010652.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #ABAP #S4HANA #TechCareers

entry.title: Demo Scenario: Clean Core Extensibility with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build
entry.content:
Co-Authors: @Sivakumar_Subramaniam , @ShivaniShankar 

A common feedback when talking about clean core extensibility is the absence of demo-ready content to visualize these guidelines that have evolved over time. This blog post attempts to take an extensibility scenario as example and showcase the equivalent scenario in a clean-core compliant world. Nevertheless, we cannot claim to be covering every possible flow in one post. The intent is to showcase one example covering a wide range. In the process, we walk through some of the decisions taken that go beyond technology and consider business aspects as well. A lot of technical details are omitted since this post is already quite long. Look forward to the discussions and feedback in the comments section to assess interest and demand for similar posts/more technical versions. 

Recommended Pre-read: 

While an effort is made to abstract technical complexity to a great extent, a reader still needs some basic understanding of the SAP ecosystem, how extensions work, why they exist and some background of clean core.

To maximize takeaways from this blog post, consider reading the clean core extensibility whitepaper before you start, particularly if "Clean Core" sounds unfamiliar. Due to the rich context, answers to why clean core etc available in the whitepaper already, we skip it here. Nevertheless, a lot of what this blog post covers would not make much sense without this background/context. 

Key Assumptions: 

1. We consider level-A of clean core extensibility since this is the highest standard. If one were to target level-B for example, the target architecture might look a little different with potential reuse of certain objects

2. For simplicity and to keep the focus on extensibility, we assume there is no fit-to-standard solution available for this scenario which may or may not be factually correct. In a real project, please consider fit-to-standard analysis and an analysis of your existing custom-developed artefacts before developing something new

Scenario Description : 
- Provide a capability to add custom gift cards when creating sales orders in SAP Cloud ERP Private (S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition)

- On applying the gift card, a discount is added to the sales order by an amount equal to the gift card amount

- On creation of a sales order, loyalty points need to get accrued against the membership of the business partner (from the sales order). For simplicity, assume only business partners of type "Customer"

- The loyalty program has tiers and based on the tier the customer is on, he/she/they accrue a percentage of the sales order value as points

- It shall be possible for a customer to request an upgrade to their loyalty program tier. In this case, approval from an admin is required before the change is active 

Scenario Refinement:  

Understanding/context: When working with sales orders, this company has 2 parallel reward programs. One, a gift card that can be applied on creation of the sales order for an immediate discount. Another, a loyalty program where one accrues points with each sales order. Potentially, for redemption (not in scope here) through different means at a later point in time

Tasks:

- Since gift cards need to be applied, there is also an unsaid need to be able to create and maintain these gift cards (by an admin). This means an app to create/maintain gift cards with appropriate fields

- To be able to apply the gift card when creating a sales order, an option is needed directly in the sales order application (or transaction)

- The applied gift card should impact the value of the sales order before creation (not post-processing) 

- Similarly, loyalty program tiers would need to be maintained (possibly as business configuration)

- The loyalty points are calculated based on the created sales order and don't have a dependency or need to update/modify the consistency of the sales order itself. This could be handled as a separate activity from the creation of the sales order

Solution Architecture (Classic Extensibility - non-Clean Core level-A):In the classic extensibility scenario which is non-clean core level-A, we use the following technology choices to build this - 

  1. Gift Card Maintenance - WebDynPro Application
  2. Capability to add gift card to sales order in VA01 (Transaction code for sales order creation) - User Exit
  3. Loyalty Management Application - Module Pool Program
  4. Tier upgrade - Classic workflow

 Solution Architecture (with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build - Clean Core)

In the clean-core level-A compliant extensibility scenario, we use the following technology choices to build this - 

  1. Gift Card Maintenance - Application developed using RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) with on-stack developer extensibility. Yes! with real code written by a developer inside the S/4HANA system outside of key user tools and still the highest level of clean core! If this is against your understanding of clean core, read SAP's official whitepaper (section 3.2 at the time this blog post is written) linked in the pre-read section of this blog post!  
  2. Capability to add gift card to sales order in the standard sales order Fiori app  - Key User Extensibility
  3. Loyalty Management Application - Side-by-side extension using BTP ABAP Environment
  4. Maintenance of tiers of the loyalty program - Business Configuration in the BTP ABAP Environment (not shown in the demo except as value help)
  5. Tier upgrade - SAP Build Process Automation
  6. Communication between S/4HANA and BTP - Event Mesh (or Advanced Event Mesh)

Alternate options - The Loyalty Points Application could also have been developed side-by-side using CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) with additional handling for tier maintenance. 

Decision Criteria (Technical and Business): 

  1. The use case around application of gift card has a very tight dependency on the sales order. The standard SAP sales order application needs to understand and bring up the gift cards for a value help, have the gift cards applied consistently across impacted business objects when the sales order is updated. All of this needs to happen as part of the sales order creation/update process in S/4HANA. This makes on-stack developer extensibility and key-user extensibility good candidates. 
  2. The loyalty points application has 2 factors to consider - Side-by-side v/s on-stack and CAP v/s ABAP Cloud
    • Side-by-Side v/s On-Stack:
      • Decision: Side-by-Side
      • In private cloud/on-premise, SAP ships a new major version every 2 years. By moving out of the core (S/4HANA) anything that can be moved logically without adverse performance/over-engineering, etc, the customer can benefit from always being on the latest release and getting the latest updates. This de-risks innovation from upgrade project timelines and upgrade strategy. 
      • Enforcement of clean core - With the side-by-side approach, clean core is enforced with syntax checks etc since ABAP Cloud is the only option on BTP ABAP Environment as opposed to governance based approaches on-stack which may need an ABAP Cloud package and processes to ensure a developer doesn't slip back to the familiar classic extensibility when cloud ready alternatives are available.
    • CAP v/s ABAP Cloud
      • Decision: Both options are valid. Here, ABAP Cloud due to developer skill set and business configuration handling know-how. Since each option has strengths and benefits, refer the BTP Developer's guide for details. To generalize any of the two as always the better option without case-to-case analysis and comparison may be an unfair/sub-optimal decision. 

General question on BTP ABAP Environment cost -
"The cost of BTP ABAP Environment is higher" - Apart from the smaller runtime size now available, which reduces costs, the cost needs to be spread out across applications being developed. One instance of the BTP ABAP Environment can be reused for multiple applications so long as the runtime/memory requirements are within available limits. Compare this to additional costs for CF Runtime (for example) for each deployed app with CAP and the costing at solution level at scale tends to be similar. This is not to say CAP is a bad option, just that when the decision is influenced by cost, how this cost is calculated decides quite a bit! When this becomes equivalent, the models can be compared on best fit for the use case on technical/architectural grounds.

Demo of functional scenario in both approaches along with ATC findings across levels - B, C, D on the package where classic extensibility was used

The following demo showcases the scenario with classic extensibility as well as with Clean Core Level-A. It also touches upon ATC checks on the package containing the classic extension. To understand how ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) helps categorize the findings, refer section 3.3.4(at the time of writing) of the clean core extensibility whitepaper (in the pre-read section)

 

Call to Action: Comment below what you would wish was better covered in the post and what key takeaways you had! Your inputs help us understand and validate where such blog posts can add more value

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Logger
entry.content:
Introduction

Logging in ABAP is one of those things that sounds boring until you've done it five times and realized you've written five different solutions. How many custom loggers have you written in your ABAP career? I've lost count.

The scenario keeps repeating. One developer needs structured messages, another insists on SLG1, and the business wants a JSON dump for a dashboard. Three projects later, and you’re deep in the FMs BAL_LOG_CREATE and BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD, maintaining a massive Z-class that is somehow totally different from the one you wrote for the last project.

Τhen came ABAP Cloud. Τhose old function modules weren't released anymore. SAP swapped out the BAL_* family for the cl_bali_* classes (like cl_bali_log and cl_bali_header_setter). The new API is better. Cleaner, properly object-oriented, makes more sense once you get it. The problem is getting it — there's a learning curve, and almost no community tooling around it yet.  Actually, the only one that I have found is this. Shoutout to the author( Björn Schulz )for the inspiration.

So, I decided to build a new one from scratch. With a few rules:

  • ABAP Cloud compatible. No legacy types, no unreleased APIs. Has to run on Steampunk and Embedded Steampunk natively.

  • One entry point for everything. Free text, symsg, exceptions, BAPIRET2 tables, or an arbitrary structure I want dumped to JSON — I shouldn't have to think about which method to call.

  • A fluent API. The kind where the code reads almost like a sentence.

  • Multiton instances keyed on object / subobject / external number. Two unrelated parts of the same transaction should be able to log to the same place without passing instances around, and without paying the cost of recreating loggers every time.

  • All those small things I keep hardcoding anyway. Built in, once.

    What came out of all this is ZCL_CLOUD_LOGGER, a fully open-source logger built specifically for ABAP Cloud.

    In this post, I’ll show you exactly how it works, dive into some of the architectural choices (and bugs I hit along the way), and explain how you can easily drop it into your own systems.

    Installation

    If you've used abapGit before, you already know the drill. If not, this is the moment to install abapGit. Ιt's the standard tool for ABAP open source distribution.

    The github project of cloud logger can be found here.

    The simplest possible example

    Once installed, here's what the absolute minimum looks like:

    DATA(log) = zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object    = 'Z_MY_LOG_OBJ'
  subobject = 'STEP_1' ).

log->log_string_add( 'Process started.' ).

" ... do stuff ...

log->log_string_add( 'Process finished.' )
   ->save_application_log( ).

    Logging anything

    The standard SAP API forces you to juggle completely different calls depending on whether you're dealing with a BAPIRET2 table, an exception class, or just a standard sy-msg. My class still gives you specific methods for those, but behind the scenes, everything is routed through the exact same core logic. This guarantees your log output stays consistent, no matter what kind of data you just tossed at it. Υou can also log arbitrary data like a serialized json.

    " A symsg-style message
log->log_message_add( symsg = VALUE #( msgty = 'E'
                                        msgid = 'ZMY_MSGS'
                                        msgno = '042'
                                        msgv1 = 'Order 100' ) ).

" An exception
TRY.
    do_something_risky( ).
  CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(err).
    log->log_exception_add( exception = err
                            severity  = 'E' ).
ENDTRY.

" Whole BAPIRET2 table — and only the errors
log->log_bapiret2_table_add( bapiret2_t   = lt_bapi_messages
                             min_severity = 'E' ).

" Log serialized to JSON automatically
log->log_data_add( data = ls_my_payload ).

    Sticky context

    Say you're looping through 50 sales orders, and each one goes through multiple validations. If one fails, your log needs to show exactly which order caused it. Without sticky context, you're forced to manually concatenate the order ID into every single string before logging it. With sticky context, you just set it once per iteration:

    log->set_context( |Order { order_id }| ).

log->log_string_add( 'validation started' ).
log->log_string_add( 'price check OK' ).
log->log_string_add( 'stock check failed' ).

log->clear_context( ).

    Built-in timing

    Every project eventually gets a requirement like "how long did this specific step take?". Since I always end up writing the same timer logic anyway, I just so I built in it directly into the logger:

    log->start_timer( ).

call_external_service( ).

log->stop_timer( 'External service call' ).

    The internal error trail

    I'll admit, almost every logger I've ever written has the same dirty secret somewhere in the code: CATCH cx_bali_runtime INTO DATA(swallowed). " ignore.You obviously don't want a simple read method like log_contains_error to trigger a dump and blow up the calling program. So, you just swallow the exception. Works fine until something actually breaks and you have no trace of what happened.

    DATA(errors) = log->get_internal_errors( ).
" Returns a table of timestamp / method_name / error_text
" for every cx_bali_runtime that was caught silently.

    Multiton config validation

    There is a specific flaw with the multiton pattern.If two callers ask for the exact same (object, subobject, ext_number) key but pass different db_save flags, the second caller just silently gets the first caller's instance. Then they waste time debugging why their db_save = abap_false log ended up being saved to the database anyway. To prevent this,  the logger checks the config and throws if there's a mismatch.

    " First caller
zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object = 'Z_LOG' subobject = 'STEP' db_save = abap_true ).

" Second caller, later in the same transaction
zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object = 'Z_LOG' subobject = 'STEP' db_save = abap_false ).
" → raises zcx_cloud_logger_error: config_mismatch

    Full Example

    Here is a full example based on a real world project:

    DATA(log) = zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object               = 'Z_INVOICE'
  subobject            = 'POSTING'
  enable_emergency_log = abap_true ).

log->set_context( |Invoice { invoice_id }| )
   ->start_timer( ).

TRY.
    post_invoice( invoice_id ).
    log->log_string_add( string = 'Posted successfully'
                         msgty  = log->c_message_type-success ).

  CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(err).
    log->log_exception_add( exception = err severity = 'E' ).
ENDTRY.

log->stop_timer( 'Invoice posting' )
   ->clear_context( )
   ->save_application_log( ).

    Conclusion

    Logging is the kind of code nobody notices when it works. The whole point of this class was to stop solving the same problem on every project and have something I can drop in and forget about. Fluent API, multiton, sticky context, timing, JSON dumps for the weird requirements — all the stuff I kept rebuilding by hand.

    The project is open source, MIT-licensed and runs in ABAP Cloud. I've worked through some bugs to get it stable, and it's now in real use. If you give it a try, tell me what's missing. Feel free to open an issue,pull request or just send me a message.Find it on GitHub.

entry.title: Pessimistic Concurrency Control (Locking) using Unmanaged Scenario in RAP
entry.content:
Introduction: 

SAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), locking has a major impact because if multiple users can update the same record simultaneously, it leads to data inconsistencies or overwrites. To work on this concurrency control, we have two different approaches-  

  1. Pessimistic concurrency control (locking). 
  2. Optimistic concurrency control (Etag or Odata Etag). 

In a RAP unmanaged scenario, pessimistic concurrency control (locking) is particularly useful because it assumes conflicts are likely and prevents them upfront by locking the record as soon as a user starts editing (like delete, update or modify operations) it. In an unmanaged scenario, the RAP framework does not automatically handle locking. Developers are responsible for implementing the entire locking mechanism manually. 

Pessimistic Concurrency Control (Locking): In RAP business objects, enqueue locks are used for pessimistic concurrency control. Exclusive locking happens on data. 

  • Exclusive Locking: Before an operation (like an UPDATE or DELETE) is executed, the RAP framework acquires an exclusive lock on the specific data instance in a global lock table. 
  • Every time a lock is requested, the system checks the lock table. If another user already holds a lock on that specific record, the new request is rejected immediately by throwing an error message. 
  •  The lock is released in the scenarios like, 
  1. ABAP session gets terminated. 
  2.  LUW gets terminated by calling commit or Rollback. 
  3.  Draft scenario is discarded or expired.

Scenario: 

In this application, we have a Sales (Header) table (ZDB_SALES_HDR) and Sales (Item) table (ZDB_SALES_ITEM) linked through so_id. Need to set the actual database lock by explicitly calling the standard Enqueue Object inside the FOR LOCK method. Users should also be able to save incomplete data as draft and complete it later. To handle custom logic and manual persistence, an unmanaged RAP approach is used. 

1. Database tables:

@EndUserText.label : 'Header sales table' 
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 
define table zdb_sales_hdr { 

key client  : abap.clnt not null; 
key so_id   : abap.char(10) not null; 
customer_id : abap.char(10); 
order_date  : sydatum; 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_sales_hdr.currency' 
total_amount: abap.curr(10,2); 
currency    : abap.cuky; 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode : ' zdb_sales_hdr.currency' 
discount_pct: abap.curr(10,2); 
status      : zde_or_status; 
created_by  : syuname; 
created_on  : timestampl; 
approved_by : syuname; 
approved_on : sydatum; 
}

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Item sales table' 
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 

define table zdb_sales_item { 
key client  : abap.clnt not null; 
key so_id   : abap.char(10) not null; 
item_no     : abap.char(10); 
material_id : abap.char(10); 
@Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure : ' zdb_sales_item.uom' 
quantity    : abap.quan(5,2); 
uom         : meins; 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode : ' zdb_sales_item.currency' 
price       : abap.curr(10,2); 
currency    : abap.cuky; 
plant       : werks_d; 
}

2. Created draft tables for both Sales (Header) and Sales (Item) entities to support draft functionality. These tables store intermediate data, allowing users to save incomplete records and activate them later.

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZI_HDR_SALES'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_header_draft {

  key mandt     : mandt not null;
  key soid      : abap.char(10) not null;
  key draftuuid : sdraft_uuid not null;
  customerid    : abap.char(10);
  orderdate     : sydatum;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_header_draft.currency'
  totalamount   : abap.curr(10,2);
  currency      : abap.cuky;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_header_draft.currency'
  discountpct   : abap.curr(10,2);
  status        : abap.char(10);
  createdby     : syuname;
  createdon     : timestampl;
  approvedby    : syuname;
  approvedon    : sydatum;
  "%admin"      : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;
}

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZI_ITEM_SALES'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
